summary: Scorching Scarlet>Fire, tight Half‑Step, explosive Terrapin jam
blurb: The May 17 1977 show delivers a blistering first set with Mississippi Half‑Step and High Time, then erupts into an iconic Terrapin Station → Playing → Wharf Rat sequence. Jerry’s tone is near‑perfect, Phil locks in on the groove, and the Sugar Magnolia encore caps a high‑energy night.
review: On May 17, 1977 at the Memorial Coliseum in Tuscaloosa, the Dead were firing on all cylinders during what many fans now call one of the Spring ’77 highlights. The first set kicked off with a driving Mississippi Half‑Step Uptown Toodeloo and rolled through bright moments like High Time and a scorching Scarlet Begonias → Fire on the Mountain that had the crowd buzzing. By the second set the band launched into a monumental Terrapin Station → Playing In The Band → Drums → Wharf Rat → Playing sandwich, a sequence repeatedly cited as one of the best May ’77 jams. The night closed with a rousing Sugar Magnolia where Bobby’s drumming shone, leaving the audience on a high note. Across all five available sources the performance is described as “tight,” “blistering,” and “legendary,” making this show a must‑listen for any Deadhead looking to experience the peak of the 1977 spring tour.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All six recordings are uniformly positive, repeatedly praising the same standout moments (Scarlet>Fire, Half‑Step, Terrapin jam) and noting no consistent performance flaws. This strong consensus warrants a high positive sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Very clear soundboard capture with good overall mix; highlights the full set and delivers the most faithful representation of the band’s tight playing.

key_highlights: Scorching Scarlet Begonias → Fire on the Mountain sequence
Explosive Terrapin Station → Playing In The Band → Wharf Rat jam
Tight, high‑energy Mississippi Half‑Step Uptown Toodeloo
Powerful Sugar Magnolia encore with Bobby’s drumming

band_performance.Jerry: Near‑perfect Travis Bean tone, soaring solos on Scarlet, Jack‑A‑Roe and Terrapin; a few reviewers note the solo on Scarlet runs a touch long but remains a highlight.
band_performance.Phil: Great bass licks on Mississippi Half‑Step, solid groove throughout, described as “on fire” and perfectly in sync with Jerry’s lead.
band_performance.Bob: Sweet vocal delivery on Jack Straw, Brown Eyed Women and harmonies; praised for melodic phrasing that blends well with Donna’s backing.
band_performance.Keith: Loose, independent playing adds a relaxed vibe; right‑on fills during Half‑Step and the big jams keep the keyboard texture vibrant.
band_performance.Mickey: Powerful, swinging drumming shines on Good Lovin’, the transition into Wharf Rat and throughout the second‑set jam flow.
band_performance.Billy: Strong, driving rhythms complement Mickey’s groove; praised for the high‑energy work on Good Lovin’ and the encore.
